quotes.topic: V to I cadence (dominant to tonic)
quotes.verbatim_quote:
We will now take chords 5, 6, 7 and 8 and form a musical cycle which resolves from V,
the dominant, to I, the tonic. In this case V to I is from Bb to Eb. Practice this chro-
matically up and down the fingerboard.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the core harmonic concept of the chapter — the dominant-to-tonic resolution — and gives a concrete key example plus a fingering instruction to practice it chromatically.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
It would do you a lot of good to analyze each of these chords to determine
what tone of the chord each note represents and which tones are omitted.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is a direct instructional rule telling the student how to engage analytically with every chord voicing, making it a technique for ear training and chord comprehension.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
There are many different ways to close a cadence but the "New Chords" in Exercises 10, 11 and
12 wil suffice for all. This is only the beginning. There are still more colorful ways of closing
a cadence.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives a scope rule — the presented voicings are sufficient for cadential closure — while flagging that additional coloristic options exist, orienting the student's expectations.
